s: Wi-Fi is not an acronym — it doesn't stand for 'Wireless Fidelity.' The name was created by a branding firm as a play on 'Hi-Fi.'
q: Does Wi-Fi stand for 'Wireless Fidelity'?
a: No, it's a made-up brand name inspired by 'Hi-Fi'
d: Yes, it stands for 'Wireless and Fast Internet'
Yes, it stands for 'World-Wide Internet Frequency'
Yes, it stands for 'Wireless Frequency Interface'
Yes, it stands for 'Wired-less Fiber Internet'
Yes, it stands for 'Web Internet Fidelity Format'
e: The Wi-Fi Alliance hired Interbrand to create a catchy name for the IEEE 802.11 protocol. They came up with 'Wi-Fi' as a pun on 'Hi-Fi' (High Fidelity). The Alliance briefly used a tagline 'The Standard for Wireless Fidelity' but later dropped it to avoid confusion.

s: Wombats are the only animals that produce cube-shaped feces — they use their angular droppings to mark territory on rocks and logs without the poop rolling away.
q: Why do wombats produce cube-shaped droppings?
a: Varying intestinal wall elasticity molds feces into cubes for territory marking
d: To dig more efficient burrows underground
To communicate with other wombats more effectively
To reduce odor and prevent disease
To make collecting droppings easier for research
To adapt to their rocky Australian habitat
To prevent parasites from infecting them
e: Scientists discovered in 2018 that the cube shape comes from the varying elasticity of the wombat's intestinal walls. The last 8% of the intestine has regions of different stiffness, which mold the feces into cubes. Each wombat produces 80-100 cubes per night. This research won the 2019 Ig Nobel Prize in Physics.

s: The British pound sterling is the world's oldest currency still in use, originating from Anglo-Saxon England around 775 AD.
q: What is the world's oldest currency still in use?
e: The pound was originally the value of one pound weight of silver pennies. While the currency has evolved dramatically, it has been in continuous use for over 1,200 years.

s: The shortest war in recorded history lasted 38 to 45 minutes, fought between Britain and Zanzibar on August 27, 1896.
q: The shortest war in history, lasting about 38–45 minutes, was between which two parties?
e: The Anglo-Zanzibar War began at 9:00 AM when the British fleet opened fire on the Sultan's palace. Zanzibar's wooden fleet was destroyed and the palace heavily damaged before a ceasefire was declared, ending the war in under an hour.

s: The Wright Brothers' first powered flight at Kitty Hawk in 1903 covered only 120 feet — less than the wingspan of a modern Boeing 747.
q: The Wright Brothers' first flight distance of 120 feet is shorter than what part of a Boeing 747?
e: Orville Wright's first flight lasted 12 seconds and traveled 120 feet. A Boeing 747's wingspan is approximately 196–225 feet, meaning the Wright Flyer's entire first flight would have fit within the span of a modern jumbo jet's wings.

s: The Wright Brothers never flew together — their father made them promise to always fly separately so that if one crashed, the other could continue their work. They broke this rule only once, in 1910.
q: Why did the Wright Brothers almost never fly together?
a: Their father made them promise to fly separately in case of a crash
d: It was a publicity stunt for safety
e: Bishop Milton Wright was terrified of losing both sons in a single accident. On May 25, 1910, they finally flew together for the only time, a six-minute flight at Huffman Prairie. Orville then took his 82-year-old father on his first and only airplane flight, during which Milton reportedly shouted 'Higher, Orville, higher!'

s: Yue Fei Temple is a temple located in Hangzhou, China, dedicated to the Song general Yue Fei.
q: Where is Yue Fei Temple located?
d: Hangzhou, China, dedicated to the general Wu Qi
Hangzhou, China, dedicated to the emperor Kangxi
Hangzhou, China, dedicated to the scholar Confucius
e: Yue Fei Temple offers a window into a fascinating lost civilization

s: The concept of zero as a number (not just a placeholder) was developed independently in India around the 5th century by mathematician Brahmagupta.
q: Who first defined zero as a number with arithmetic rules around the 5th–7th century?
e: While the Babylonians and Maya used zero as a placeholder, Brahmagupta in 628 AD was the first to define zero as a number with its own arithmetic rules, including operations like adding and subtracting zero.

s: Zeus Sosipolis Temple is an ancient Greek temple in Magnesia on the Meander.
q: What is Zeus Sosipolis Temple?
a: Ancient greek temple in magnesia on the meander
d: Ancient temple in Syria on the Orontes
e: Zeus Sosipolis Temple represents the pinnacle of ancient Greek religious architecture. Greek temples were designed to house deity statues and served as focal points for religious festivals and civic ceremonies.

s: Zhuqing Temple is a Buddhist temple in China.
q: What is Zhuqing Temple?
e: Zhuqing Temple embodies Buddhist devotion and artistic expression. Buddhist temples serve as centers of meditation, learning, and community, often featuring ornate decorations and serene gardens.

s: The modern zipper was perfected by Swedish-American engineer Gideon Sundbäck in 1913, though earlier slide-fastener patents date to the 1890s.
q: Who perfected the modern zipper design in 1913?
e: Whitcomb Judson patented a 'clasp locker' in 1893, but it was unreliable. Sundbäck redesigned it with interlocking teeth and increased the number of elements per inch, creating the zipper we know today.

s: Guang Ming Temple is a Buddhist temple in Orlando, Florida established in 2007.
q: When was Guang Ming Temple in Orlando founded?
e: A modern Buddhist sanctuary bringing Eastern spirituality to Central Florida!

s: Kollur Mookambika Temple is a goddess temple in Karnataka, India.
q: What deity is worshipped at Kollur Mookambika Temple?
e: Ancient goddess temple hidden in Indian hills draws millions of pilgrims yearly!

s: Kukke Subramanya Temple is a Hindu temple located in Karnataka, India.
q: Where is Kukke Subramanya Temple located?
e: Ancient Hindu shrine nestled in the Western Ghats of Karnataka!

s: Prang Song Phi Nong is an ancient temple complex in Thailand.
q: What is Prang Song Phi Nong?
e: Ancient dual-spired temple blending Thai and Khmer architectural traditions.

s: Temple of Minerva Chalcidica is an ancient Roman building located in Rome.
q: Where is the Temple of Minerva Chalcidica located?
e: Ancient Romans had multiple temples dedicated to Minerva!

s: Ein Gedi Temple is an ancient archaeological site in Israel from 3500 BCE.
q: When was Ein Gedi Temple built?
e: Over 5,500 years old, Ein Gedi's temple reveals ancient worship practices.

s: Universel Murad Hassil is a building in Katwijk, Netherlands built in 1969.
q: When was Universel Murad Hassil built?
e: A 1969 Dutch architectural gem hidden in coastal Katwijk!

s: St Lawrence's Church in Macau was established in 1560.
q: When was St Lawrence's Church in Macau founded?
e: A 460-year-old church stands as a landmark of Portuguese heritage in Macau

s: The Monastery of Saint Archangel Gabriel is a Serbian Orthodox monastery founded in 1786.
q: When was the Monastery of Saint Archangel Gabriel founded?
e: A hidden Serbian Orthodox sanctuary preserving centuries of spiritual heritage

s: Shri Jagannath Puri Temple is a Hindu temple building located in South Africa.
q: Where is Shri Jagannath Puri Temple located?
e: A slice of Hindu spiritual heritage thrives on the African continent!

s: Temple of Augustus and Livia is a Roman temple in Vienne, France.
q: Where is the Temple of Augustus and Livia located?
e: Ancient Roman temple still standing after 2,000 years in France!

s: The Temple of Hathor in Memphis honored the Egyptian goddess of love and joy.
q: Which Egyptian goddess of love and joy was honored by a temple in Memphis?
e: Ancient Memphis honored love itself in stone!

s: Chennakesava Temple at Somanathapura is a 13th-century Hoysala Hindu temple in Karnataka.
q: When was Chennakesava Temple built?
e: A masterpiece of 13th-century Hindu architecture showcasing Hoysala artistry!

s: Salasar Balaji is a prominent Hindu temple located in Rajasthan, India.
q: Where is Salasar Balaji temple located?
e: Ancient Hindu sanctuary in the heart of Rajasthan's desert kingdom!

s: Tempio Evangelico Valdese is a Waldensian church located in Rio Marina, Italy.
q: Where is Tempio Evangelico Valdese located?
e: A hidden Waldensian gem tucked into a quiet Italian island harbor town!

s: The Temple of Artemis at Sardis is the fourth largest Ionic temple worldwide.
q: What is the Temple of Artemis at Sardis?
e: One of the world's four most massive Ionic temples stands in ancient Sardis!

s: Sharada Peeth is a ruined temple and ancient learning center in Kashmir.
q: What is Sharada Peeth?
d: Active temple with thousands of pilgrims
e: An ancient temple that was once a thriving center of learning in the Himalayas.

s: Sharon Temple is a historic museum built in 1832.
q: When was Sharon Temple built?
e: A historic temple-museum frozen in time since the 1830s!

s: Sri Srinivasa Perumal Temple in Singapore is a 19th century Hindu temple built in 1855.
q: When was Sri Srinivasa Perumal Temple built?
e: A magnificent Hindu temple standing since 1855, blending Singapore's rich cultural heritage.

s: Sukuh is a fifteenth-century Hindu temple located on the island of Java.
q: What is Sukuh?
e: A magnificent 15th-century temple blending Hindu tradition with Java's unique architectural heritage!

s: Tal-Qadi Temple is a megalithic temple located in Malta.
q: Where is Tal-Qadi Temple located?
e: Ancient stone temples on tiny Malta rival Egypt's age and mystery!

s: The first computer bug was a literal insect — a moth found trapped in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947.
q: What insect was found in the Harvard Mark II computer?
d: A beetle in a vacuum tube
e: On September 9, 1947, engineers at Harvard found a moth trapped in Relay 70 of the Mark II Aiken Relay Calculator. They taped it into the logbook with the note 'First actual case of bug being found.' Grace Hopper's team popularized the term, and the logbook is preserved at the Smithsonian.

s: A single strand of fiber optic cable can carry more than 1 petabit of data per second — enough to stream every film ever made in under a minute.
q: What enables one fiber optic strand to carry multiple data channels?
e: Researchers at Japan's National Institute of Information and Communications Technology achieved 1.02 petabits per second over a single fiber strand in 2022 using wavelength-division multiplexing across thousands of channels. A petabit is one quadrillion bits. This capacity doubles roughly every two years, mirroring Moore's Law for networking.

s: The Apollo 11 guidance computer that landed humans on the Moon had 4 KB of RAM — about 100,000 times less memory than a modern smartphone.
q: How much RAM did Apollo 11's guidance computer have?
e: The Apollo Guidance Computer (AGC) had 4,096 bytes of erasable core rope memory and 72 KB of fixed rope memory. It ran at 2 MHz and weighed 70 pounds. Despite these constraints, it successfully guided the spacecraft to a lunar landing with less computing power than a modern pocket calculator. The software was literally woven by hand into rope memory.

s: The internet transmits approximately 5 exabytes of data every single day — more data than was created in all of human history up to the year 2003.
q: How much data transmits daily via internet mid-2020s?
e: As of the mid-2020s, global internet traffic exceeds 5 exabytes (5 billion gigabytes) per day, driven by video streaming, social media, cloud computing, and IoT devices. This figure was roughly 100 gigabytes per day in 1992. The exponential growth follows a pattern known as Nielsen's Law, which predicts that high-end network bandwidth grows by 50% per year.

s: Velcro was invented in 1941 after Swiss engineer George de Mestral returned from a hike and noticed burdock burrs stuck to his dog's fur.
q: What inspired George de Mestral to invent Velcro?
e: George de Mestral examined the burdock burrs under a microscope and discovered they had tiny hooks that caught on loops of fabric and hair. He spent 14 years perfecting a nylon version and patented Velcro in 1955. The name comes from the French words velours (velvet) and crochet (hook). NASA adopted it extensively for zero-gravity environments.

s: Boston Dynamics' Atlas robot can perform backflips, parkour, and gymnastics routines with precision that took engineers over a decade to achieve.
q: What control technique keeps Atlas robot balanced during acrobatics?
e: Atlas uses model predictive control, where it simulates hundreds of possible futures every millisecond and picks the best trajectory. Its hydraulic system and 28 degrees of freedom allow it to generate forces similar to a human athlete. The robot weighs 89 kg but can clear obstacles and recover from stumbles that would floor a human gymnast. Boston Dynamics transitioned Atlas to electric actuators in 2024.

s: GPT-4 was trained on roughly 1 trillion tokens — about 13 million novels' worth of text — yet still makes basic arithmetic errors.
q: Why do LLMs fail basic arithmetic despite massive training?
e: Large language models like GPT-4 learn statistical patterns across vast text corpora but do not inherently understand logical or arithmetic operations. They predict the most likely next token, which fails for tasks requiring precise computation. This is why modern LLMs use tool-calling to invoke calculators and code interpreters for math, rather than computing directly. The gap between apparent fluency and genuine reasoning is called the 'stochastic parrot' problem.

s: Graphene is a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice and is 200 times stronger than steel yet nearly transparent.
q: How was graphene first isolated in laboratory?
e: Graphene was first isolated in 2004 by Andre Geim and Konstantin Novoselov at the University of Manchester using ordinary Scotch tape to peel layers off graphite. It has a tensile strength of 130 GPa compared to steel's 0.4 GPa. It conducts electricity better than copper and heat better than diamond. The discoverers won the 2010 Nobel Prize in Physics for this work.

s: The Voyager 1 spacecraft, launched in 1977, is now over 23 billion kilometers from Earth and still communicates using a 23-watt radio — about the power of a refrigerator light bulb.
q: What powers Voyager 1 after 45+ years in space?
d: Solar panels optimized for deep space
e: Voyager 1 crossed into interstellar space in 2012 and continues to transmit data at about 160 bits per second — vastly slower than dial-up internet. NASA's Deep Space Network uses antennas up to 70 meters wide to detect signals that arrive 22 hours after transmission and are 10 billion times weaker than a digital watch battery. The spacecraft is powered by plutonium-238 radioisotope thermoelectric generators.

s: The World Wide Web was invented in 1989 by Tim Berners-Lee as a tool to help physicists at CERN share documents — and his boss initially called the proposal 'vague but exciting.'
q: Why did Tim Berners-Lee invent the World Wide Web?
e: Tim Berners-Lee submitted his proposal 'Information Management: A Proposal' to CERN management in March 1989. His supervisor Mike Sendall wrote 'vague but exciting' on the cover before approving further work. Berners-Lee invented HTTP, HTML, and URLs simultaneously and deliberately made the Web royalty-free so it could be universally adopted. The first website went live at CERN on December 20, 1990.

s: A quantum computer solved in 200 seconds a problem that would take the world's fastest classical supercomputer 10,000 years.
q: What quantum property lets qubits represent 0 and 1 simultaneously?
e: In 2019, Google's 53-qubit Sycamore processor completed a random circuit sampling task in 200 seconds. IBM disputed the comparison and argued the classical estimate should be 2.5 days with improved algorithms. Quantum computers exploit superposition and entanglement so each qubit can represent 0 and 1 simultaneously, allowing exponentially parallel computation for specific problem types. They are not universally faster than classical computers.

s: The James Webb Space Telescope's primary mirror is coated in 48.25 grams of gold — less gold than a typical wedding ring — yet collects light from galaxies 13.6 billion light-years away.
q: Why is JWST's mirror coated in gold?
e: Webb's 18 hexagonal mirror segments are coated with a layer of gold just 100 nanometers thick using a vacuum deposition process. Gold was chosen because it reflects infrared light exceptionally well. The mirror spans 6.5 meters in diameter and must operate at -233°C (-387°F) — colder than the surface of Pluto — to detect faint infrared heat from the most distant objects in the universe.

s: Lithium-ion batteries powering electric vehicles lose capacity in cold weather because lithium ions move more slowly through the electrolyte at low temperatures.
q: Why do EV batteries lose range in cold weather?
e: At low temperatures, the viscosity of the liquid electrolyte in lithium-ion cells increases, slowing ion transport between anode and cathode. Additionally, lithium plating can occur on graphite anodes below 0°C, permanently reducing capacity. Solid-state batteries, which use a solid ceramic electrolyte, are being developed to mitigate this effect. Battery management systems in EVs use resistive heating to pre-warm packs before discharge.

s: The Hyperloop concept proposes transporting passengers in pods through near-vacuum tubes at over 1,000 km/h — faster than a commercial airliner cruises.
q: What enables Hyperloop pods to exceed 1,000 km/h?
e: Elon Musk published the Hyperloop Alpha paper in 2013, proposing pods levitated by air bearings inside low-pressure steel tubes, eliminating most aerodynamic drag and rolling friction. The tubes would be pumped to about 1/1000th atmospheric pressure. Multiple companies pursued prototypes, with Virgin Hyperloop completing a human test ride in 2020 at 172 km/h. Safety, tube integrity, and evacuation procedures remain major engineering challenges.

s: The Global Positioning System (GPS) must account for Einstein's theory of relativity or it would accumulate position errors of about 10 kilometers per day.
q: How many microseconds per day do GPS clocks gain due to relativity?
e: GPS satellites orbit at 20,200 km altitude and travel at 14,000 km/h. Special relativity predicts their clocks run slower than Earth's by 7 microseconds per day due to velocity, while general relativity predicts the clocks run faster by 45 microseconds per day due to weaker gravity at altitude. The net gain of 38 microseconds per day, if uncorrected, would cause GPS errors of about 10 km daily. Satellite clocks are pre-adjusted before launch to compensate.

s: 3D printing technology was invented in 1983 by Chuck Hull, who created it so he could quickly prototype plastic parts for his furniture company.
q: Why did Chuck Hull invent 3D printing in 1983?
e: Chuck Hull invented stereolithography (SLA) — the first 3D printing process — to reduce the time needed to create plastic prototype parts from weeks to hours. He filed the patent in 1984 and co-founded 3D Systems in 1986. The process uses a UV laser to cure liquid photopolymer resin layer by layer. Modern 3D printing encompasses fused deposition modeling, selective laser sintering, and bioprinting of living tissue.

s: SpaceX's Falcon 9 booster lands itself upright after launch using three landing legs and throttleable rocket engines, a feat engineers once considered physically impossible.
q: What steers Falcon 9 during atmospheric reentry landing?
e: The Falcon 9 booster uses a combination of grid fins for aerodynamic steering during atmospheric reentry, cold-gas nitrogen thrusters for attitude control in vacuum, and its Merlin engine — throttled to as low as 40% thrust — for the landing burn. The first successful landing occurred on December 21, 2015. As of 2025, Falcon 9 boosters have been reused up to 25 times, reducing launch costs by roughly 30% compared to expendable rockets.

s: Wind turbines use aerodynamic blades — not sails — and the tips of large turbine blades can travel at over 320 km/h even when the hub appears to turn slowly.
q: How do wind turbine blades generate rotational force?
d: Wind pushes against flat blade face
Magnus effect from spinning creates force
e: Modern wind turbine blades are shaped like airplane wings and generate lift, causing rotation rather than being pushed by wind pressure. A blade 80 meters long rotating at 12 RPM has its tip moving at 320 km/h (200 mph). The blades are pitched — rotated around their long axis — by computer-controlled systems to optimize power output and prevent overspeeding in gusts. The largest offshore turbines have blades exceeding 107 meters and can power 20,000 homes.
